questions: In the context of U.S. federal education rights for students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), identify the primary statute that mandates the provision of 'special education and related services' through an individualized plan, distinct from civil rights laws that solely prevent discrimination. According to this specific act, under which official disability category are students with ADHD most frequently classified to receive services, and what is the full name of the legal document that must be developed to outline their educational goals?

In the context of educational rights for students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in the United States, identify the primary federal statute that governs how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services. Do not simply name the law; you must identify it based on its specific mandate for 'special education' as opposed to broader civil rights protections. Once this legislation is identified, specify the particular disability category under which ADHD is most frequently classified for eligibility purposes within this act, and provide the full formal name and acronym of the law.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
rounds.answer_a:
The primary federal statute that mandates provision of “special education and related services” through an individualized plan is the **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)**, most recently reauthorized as the Individuals with Disabilities Education Improvement Act of 2004.[1][2] Unlike Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act or the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), which are primarily anti-discrimination civil rights laws, IDEA is an education entitlement law: it requires eligible children with disabilities to receive a free appropriate public education (FAPE) via specially designed instruction and related services.[1]

Under IDEA’s eligibility categories, students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) are most often served under the category of **“Other Health Impairment” (OHI)**.[1][3] The statute and regulations define “other health impairment” to include conditions that result in limited alertness due to chronic or acute health problems—explicitly naming ADHD as an example—when these adversely affect educational performance and require special education and related services.[2][3]

The formal legal document that must be developed for each eligible student under IDEA is the **Individualized Education Program (IEP)**.[1][2] The IEP is a written plan created by a legally required team (including parents) that specifies the child’s present levels of academic and functional performance, measurable annual goals, special education and related services, accommodations, and how progress will be measured.[2]

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education, “A Guide to the Individualized Education Program,” and IDEA overview pages.  
[2] 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq.; 34 C.F.R. Part 300 (IDEA regulations, definition of OHI and IEP requirements).  
[3] U.S. Department of Education, Office for Civil Rights & OSERS, “Students with ADHD and Section 504: A Resource Guide” (discussion of IDEA/OHI classification).
rounds.answer_b:
The primary federal statute mandating special education and related services for students with ADHD is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), originally enacted in 1975 as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act and reauthorized multiple times, most recently in 2004 as the Individuals with Disabilities Education Improvement Act.

Under IDEA, students with ADHD are most frequently classified under the disability category "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) to receive special education services. The IDEA regulations specifically define OHI as having "limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" due to chronic or acute health problems, with ADHD explicitly listed as a qualifying condition [1][2].

The legal document that must be developed to outline educational goals for qualifying students is the Individualized Education Program (IEP). The IEP is a comprehensive written statement developed by a multidisciplinary team including parents, teachers, special education professionals, and when appropriate, the student. This legally binding document details the student's present levels of academic achievement and functional performance, establishes measurable annual goals, specifies the special education and related services to be provided, and outlines how progress will be measured [3][4].

It's important to note that IDEA differs from Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, which is a civil rights law preventing discrimination but doesn't require the comprehensive special education services and IEP development mandated by IDEA. While both laws can serve students with ADHD, IDEA provides more extensive educational support through the IEP process [5].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2022). IDEA Section 300.8 Child with a disability. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/regs/b/a/300.8

[2] Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2024). Educational Services for Children with ADHD. https://www.cdc.gov/adhd/treatment/school-treatment.html

[3] U.S. Department of Education. (2017). A Guide to the Individualized Education Program. https://www2.ed.gov/parents/needs/speced/iepguide/index.html

[4] Understood.org. (2024). What is an IEP? https://www.understood.org/articles/what-is-iep

[5] U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights. (2020). Protecting Students with Disabilities. https://www2.ed.gov/about/offices/list/ocr/504faq.html

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]:  [Wide Logic - Target 2]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
rounds.answer_a:
The primary federal statute is the **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)**, a special‑education entitlement law that requires states and public agencies to provide **early intervention, special education, and related services** to eligible children with disabilities, rather than merely prohibiting discrimination.[1][2] Unlike Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act or the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), which focus on equal access and non‑discrimination, IDEA specifically mandates that eligible students receive a **free appropriate public education (FAPE)** through specially designed instruction and educational supports detailed in an individualized plan.[1][2]

IDEA governs both **early intervention services** for infants and toddlers (Part C) and **special education and related services** for school‑aged children (Part B).[2] To qualify, a child must meet criteria under one of the statute’s defined “disability categories” and need special education as a result.[2]

For students with **Attention‑Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)**, eligibility under IDEA is most commonly established using the category **“Other Health Impairment” (OHI)**.[1][3] Federal regulations define “other health impairment” as having limited strength, vitality, or alertness— including heightened alertness to environmental stimuli—due to chronic or acute health problems such as ADHD, which adversely affects a child’s educational performance.[2][3] When ADHD substantially impacts learning or school functioning and requires specially designed instruction, students are typically served under this OHI category, even though ADHD can sometimes intersect with other categories (e.g., specific learning disability).

The law’s **full formal name and acronym** are:

**Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)**, codified at **20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq.**[2]

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education, “A Guide to the Individualized Education Program,” ed.gov.  
[2] 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq.; 34 C.F.R. Part 300 (IDEA regulations, including definitions and eligibility).  
[3] U.S. Department of Education, OSERS/OCR, “Students with ADHD and Section 504: A Resource Guide,” ed.gov.

The primary federal statute governing special education services for students with ADHD is the **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)**, formally known as the Individuals with Disabilities Education Improvement Act of 2004. This law specifically mandates that states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to eligible children with disabilities, distinguishing it from civil rights laws like Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act that focus solely on preventing discrimination [1][2].

IDEA requires the provision of a "free appropriate public education" (FAPE) in the least restrictive environment, along with procedural safeguards and individualized education planning. This comprehensive framework goes beyond mere non-discrimination by actively requiring specialized instruction and support services tailored to each child's unique needs [2][3].

Under IDEA, students with ADHD are most frequently classified under the disability category of **"Other Health Impairment" (OHI)**. The federal regulations define OHI as "having limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" due to chronic or acute health problems [1][4]. ADHD is explicitly listed as a qualifying condition under this category when it adversely affects educational performance.

The OHI classification recognizes ADHD as a neurological condition that impacts attention regulation and executive functioning in educational settings. For a student with ADHD to qualify under IDEA, the condition must substantially limit one or more major life activities and require special education services, not just accommodations [3][5]. This classification enables students to receive comprehensive support through an Individualized Education Program (IEP), which outlines specific educational goals, services, and progress monitoring strategies tailored to address the unique challenges posed by ADHD in the academic environment.

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2024). IDEA Section 300.8(c)(9) - Other Health Impairment Definition. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/regs/b/a/300.8

[2] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). About IDEA. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/about-idea/

[3] Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2024). ADHD and School. https://www.cdc.gov/adhd/treatment/school-treatment.html

[4] National Center for Learning Disabilities. (2024). ADHD and IDEA: Other Health Impairment Category. https://www.ncld.org/news/policy-and-advocacy/adhd-idea-category/

[5] CHADD - Children and Adults with ADHD. (2024). Educational Rights for Children with ADHD. https://chadd.org/for-parents/educational-rights/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the statute (IDEA) and the eligibility category (Other Health Impairment/OHI). Agent A is preferred for two main reasons: 
1. **Structure & Directness**: The prompt specifically asked to provide the full formal name and acronym. Agent A separated this into a clear, distinct section at the end, making it immediately verifiable, whereas Agent B buried it in the first paragraph.
2. **Legal Precision**: Agent A included the specific U.S. Code citation (20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq.), which adds significant value and precision to a query about federal statutes.

Agent B included raw search artifacts (XML tags) in the response, which negatively impacts the user experience if visible, and its answer was slightly less structured regarding the specific constraints.
